21.text: RoPE changes how position is represented inside attention, not the surrounding training loop, so the setup remains intentionally familiar.

22.text: Use the same shared corpus so the only meaningful change is how position enters the model.

23.text: Fix the training order so any behavioral change comes from rotary position encoding, not from a different run.

25.text: PyTorch handles the mechanics; the new idea in this lab is geometric, not infrastructural.

26.text: Same transformer building blocks as before. That makes RoPE easier to isolate as an attention-level upgrade rather than a full architectural rewrite.

27.text: Softmax and loss stay ordinary. The interesting difference is where positional information enters the computation.

29.text: Lock both RNGs so the before-and-after story stays about position handling instead of run-to-run noise.

30.text: PyTorch gets its own seed because initialization is part of the experiment too.

35.text: Portable path to the shared dataset.

36.text: If the dataset file is missing, we will download it automatically. This makes the script self-contained so you can run it without manual setup.

37.text: Lazy import, only when the file needs downloading.

39.text: The raw URL for Karpathy's names dataset. About 32K human names that serve as our training corpus across all labs.

40.text: Fetch the file from the internet once and save it locally. After the first download, every future run loads instantly from disk.

42.text: Read all names from disk, strip whitespace, and drop blank lines. Each name becomes one training document for next-token prediction.

43.text: Shuffle so the model doesn't learn alphabetical order.

45.text: Unique sorted characters become the base vocabulary.

46.text: BOS token gets the next available id.

53.text: Embedding dimension, small enough to train quickly, large enough to see RoPE's effect.

54.text: 4 attention heads, each operating on its own subspace.

55.text: Single layer keeps focus on the positional encoding change.

56.text: Maximum sequence length, names are short.

63.text: Precompute the rotation frequencies once, they never change during training or inference.

69.text: Pair up dimensions: indices 0,2,4,... Each pair will be rotated together as a 2D vector.

70.text: The frequency for each pair: theta_i = 1/10000^(2i/dim). Lower pairs rotate fast (local patterns), higher pairs rotate slowly (global patterns). 10000 is the standard base from the RoPE paper.

71.text: Position indices: 0, 1, 2, ... Each position gets a different rotation angle.

72.text: Outer product gives a 2D table: angles[pos][pair] = how much to rotate dimension-pair pair at position pos.

73.text: Return cos and sin, the two components of the rotation matrix. Precomputed once, reused every forward pass.

76.text: Apply the rotation to query or key tensors. This is where position information enters the attention computation.

82.text: Grab the sequence length to slice the precomputed frequencies.

83.text: Broadcast cos frequencies to match the (batch, heads, time, pairs) shape.

85.text: Split each head's dimensions into even-indexed (x1) and odd-indexed (x2), these form 2D rotation pairs.

86.text: The paired dimension, together with x1, this forms a 2D vector that gets rotated.

87.text: 2D rotation formula, real part: x1*cos - x2*sin. Same as rotating a point in 2D space.

88.text: 2D rotation formula, imaginary part: x1*sin + x2*cos. The angle encodes the absolute position.

89.text: Interleave rotated pairs back into the original dimension ordering. The key insight: when Q at position m and K at position n are dot-producted, the rotation angles subtract, making attention depend on m-n (relative distance) automatically.

93.text: Compute once at startup. These frequency tables are shared by all layers and all forward passes.

99.text: RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm, simpler (no mean subtraction), works just as well. Used by LLaMA and most modern LLMs.

100.text: Dimension parameter stored for documentation; RMSNorm doesn't actually need it.

102.text: A tiny epsilon (1e-5) added inside the square root to prevent division by zero when activations happen to be near-zero.

105.text: Normalize by root-mean-square of activations. No learned scale parameter in this minimal version.

108.text: Attention module with a flag to switch between learned positional embeddings and RoPE.

109.text: The use_rope flag lets us run a fair A/B comparison with identical architecture otherwise.

111.text: Store the flag so forward knows which position strategy to use.

112.text: Query projection, same for both variants.

113.text: Key projection, same weights, but RoPE will rotate these before the dot product.

114.text: Value projection, notably, RoPE does NOT touch values.

115.text: Output projection merges heads back together.

117.text: The attention forward pass takes a full input tensor and produces contextualized output. RoPE rotation happens internally before the dot product.

118.text: Unpack batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension.

119.text: Project to queries, reshape into heads: (B, T, C) -> (B, n_head, T, head_dim).

120.text: Keys get the same per-head reshape as queries. Each head's keys will only be compared against that same head's queries.

121.text: Same for values, but values won't be rotated even with RoPE.

123.text: Branch: if RoPE is enabled, rotate Q and K by position-dependent angles.

124.text: Rotate queries, each position gets a unique rotation angle.

125.text: Rotate keys, when Q and K are dot-producted, the rotation angles subtract to give relative position.

129.text: Upper-triangular mask: each position can only attend to itself and earlier positions (causal/autoregressive).

130.text: Set future positions to -infinity so softmax gives them zero weight.

133.text: Weighted sum of values, reshape back from heads to embedding dimension.

134.text: Output projection, mix information across heads.

137.text: Feed-forward network: expand to 4x, apply nonlinearity, project back.

140.text: Expand to 4x the embedding dimension, the "hidden" layer of the MLP.

141.text: Project back down to embedding dimension.

143.text: ReLU activation between the two linear layers.

144.text: Two-layer MLP with ReLU: the pattern-matching complement to attention's pattern-finding.

147.text: Transformer block: attention + MLP, each with pre-norm and residual connection.

148.text: Thread the RoPE flag down to the attention layer so it knows whether to apply rotary position encoding or rely on the learned position embeddings.

150.text: Pre-norm before attention (LLaMA-style, more stable than post-norm).

151.text: Attention layer, configured for either learned positions or RoPE.

152.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network. Like the pre-attention norm, this stabilizes training by keeping activations on a consistent scale.

153.text: The position-independent feed-forward network. After attention has mixed information across positions, the MLP transforms each position's representation individually.

155.text: Forward: residual connections let gradients flow straight through.

161.text: The full model, identical architecture for both variants, differing only in positional encoding.

162.text: Constructor takes use_rope to choose the position strategy.

164.text: Remember whether this model uses RoPE so the forward pass knows whether to add learned position embeddings or skip them entirely.

165.text: Token embedding table: maps each of 27 token ids to a 16-dimensional vector.

166.text: Only create learned positional embeddings when NOT using RoPE, this is the parameter saving.

167.text: Learned position embeddings: one vector per position. RoPE eliminates this entire table.

169.text: Stack transformer blocks, each configured with the same position strategy.

170.text: Output projection: from embedding space back to vocabulary logits.

171.text: Walk through every submodule and apply the custom weight initializer. This ensures consistent starting conditions whether the module is a linear layer or an embedding.

174.text: Small random initialization (std=0.08) for all linear and embedding weights.

176.text: Normal distribution init, not too large (exploding), not too small (vanishing).

178.text: Forward pass: embed, add positions (if learned), normalize, transform, project to logits.

180.text: Convert each input token ID into a dense 16-dimensional vector. These embeddings are the model's learned representation of each character.

181.text: Only add learned positional embeddings for the baseline variant.

182.text: Add position vectors to token embeddings, the learned-position approach from labs 01-08.

184.text: Send the representation through each transformer block in sequence. Each block first applies attention (with or without RoPE) then the MLP.

186.text: Project to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

192.text: Shared training function, both models train with identical hyperparameters for a fair comparison.

194.text: Adam optimizer with slightly aggressive betas for small-model training.

196.text: Train for 1000 steps, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

197.text: Cycle through names. With 32K names and 1000 steps, we see ~3% of the data.

199.text: Clip to block_size to handle names longer than 16 characters.

201.text: Input: all tokens except the last.

202.text: Target: all tokens shifted by one, the next-token prediction task.

205.text: Cross-entropy loss: how surprised is the model by the correct next token?

207.text: Zero out gradients from the previous step.

208.text: Backpropagate the loss through the entire computation graph.

209.text: Linear learning rate warmdown: start at 1e-2, linearly decay to 0.

210.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every optimizer parameter group so the next weight update takes a smaller step as training progresses.

221.text: Generate names autoregressively, one token at a time, feeding each back as input.

223.text: Switch to eval mode (disables dropout, etc.).

225.text: No gradient computation needed during generation.

227.text: Start with just the BOS token.

229.text: Feed the current sequence (up to block_size) through the model.

230.text: Get logits for the last position, the model's prediction for the next token.

231.text: Divide by temperature: lower = more confident/repetitive, higher = more random/creative.

232.text: Turn raw logits into a proper probability distribution via softmax. Sampling from these probabilities gives us the next token.

233.text: Sample from the distribution, stochastic generation.

234.text: If we sampled BOS, the name is complete.

236.text: Add the sampled token and continue generating.

237.text: Decode token ids back to characters.

243.text: Train the baseline first: standard learned positional embeddings, same as labs 01-08.

244.text: Reset seed so both models start from the same random initialization.

245.text: Baseline model with learned position embeddings.

246.text: Count parameters, the baseline has extra parameters for the position embedding table.

248.text: Train the baseline and capture loss history.

250.text: Now train the RoPE variant with the same seed and data order.

251.text: Same random init for a fair comparison.

252.text: RoPE model, no learned position embeddings, positions encoded through rotation.

253.text: Count parameters, fewer than baseline because there's no wpe embedding table.

254.text: Show the parameter saving: RoPE encodes position for free (no learned parameters).

255.text: Train RoPE variant with identical hyperparameters.

259.text: Moving average of recent losses smooths out step-to-step noise.

260.text: Average the last 50 losses for each model.

261.text: Same window for the RoPE model.

265.text: Generate names from the learned-position model.

266.text: Generate names from the RoPE model, quality should be comparable despite fewer parameters.
